name: Perun
description: Perun stands as the supreme god of the Slavic pantheon, embodying the power of thunder, lightning, storms, and war. He is often depicted as a robust warrior, armed with a mighty hammer or axe, riding a chariot pulled by goats, and dwelling atop the highest peak of the sacred mountains. As a sky god, Perun is responsible for maintaining cosmic order and dispensing justice. His battles against Veles, the god of the underworld and chaos, represent the eternal struggle between good and evil, order and disorder, a fundamental duality reflected in the natural world. Perun's influence permeated various aspects of Slavic life, from agriculture, where he brought life-giving rain, to warfare, where he granted victory to those he favored. He was revered as a protector of warriors and a symbol of strength, his name invoked in oaths and rituals aimed at securing his favor and protection. Sacrifices, often involving oxen or other livestock, were offered to appease him and ensure his continued blessings.
subtitle: The Thunderer

keyMyths.title: Perun vs. Veles
keyMyths.description: The eternal battle between Perun and Veles is a central myth in Slavic cosmology. Veles, god of the underworld, cattle, and magic, steals Perun's cattle, children, or wife (depending on the version), causing drought and chaos. Perun, enraged, pursues Veles across the heavens and earth, striking him with lightning bolts. Veles is eventually defeated and forced back into the underworld, restoring order and bringing rain to the land. This myth symbolizes the cyclical nature of the seasons and the ongoing conflict between order and chaos.
keyMyths.source: Gieysztor, Aleksander. *Mitologia Słowian*. Wydawnictwa Artystyczne i Filmowe, 1982.

keyMyths.title: The Sacred Oak
keyMyths.description: Oak trees were considered sacred to Perun, representing strength, longevity, and connection to the divine. Groves of oak trees were often dedicated to him, serving as sites for worship and sacrifice. It was believed that Perun's lightning would strike oak trees more often than other trees, further solidifying their association with him. The oak's deep roots symbolized Perun's power extending into the earth, while its towering height connected him to the sky. Oaths were sworn under oak trees, invoking Perun as a witness and guarantor of truth.
keyMyths.source: Иванов, Вячеслав Всеволодович, and Владимир Николаевич Топоров. *Исследования в области славянских древностей*. Наука, 1974.

keyMyths.title: Perun's Judgement
keyMyths.description: Perun was not only a god of war but also a dispenser of justice. He was believed to punish those who broke oaths, committed perjury, or otherwise violated the moral code. His lightning bolts were seen as divine retribution, striking down the wicked and upholding righteousness. In some accounts, he is associated with the establishment of laws and customs, ensuring order and stability within the community. His role as a judge reflected the importance of honor and integrity in Slavic society.
keyMyths.source: Niederle, Lubor. *Slovanské Starožitnosti*. Nákladem Bursíka & Kohouta, 1911.

sources.author: Gieysztor, Aleksander
sources.description: A comprehensive overview of Slavic mythology, covering various deities, myths, and rituals.
